text: In this video, we present the main steps to perform a pure robotic left hemipatectomy.
The image shows the relative positioning of the patient, surgeons, and the surgical robot in the operating room.
The extra-glycinean approach is preferred for anatomic hepatectomy.
As in laparoscopic surgery, in robotic surgery the Pringle maneuver is mandatory and must be prepared to prevent and manage bleeding problems during the course of surgery.
The use of the Wong loop is very useful and very easy to do with robotic devices. To do this, we use a urinary catheter.
The surgeon divides the round ligament of the liver, followed by the suspensory ligament, progressing towards the line of the hepatic veins.
The surgeon divides the left triangular ligament of the liver, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the ligament from surrounding structures.
The surgeon releases the right triangular ligament, exposing the edge of the right hepatic vein.
The surgeon prepares the extra-glycinean approach to the left hilar pedicle, focusing on the anatomical structures surrounding the pedicle for precise dissection.
The surgeon raises the lower face of the liver and opens the lesser omentum, progressing towards the aranchium ligament.
The surgeon uses blunt maneuvers to detach the Lien X capsule from surrounding tissues, following the techniques described by Takasaki and Sujika.
The surgeon identifies Gate III, a specific anatomical landmark, during the procedure.
The surgeon isolates the left hilar pedicle and temporarily clamps it, creating a visible ischemic zone to facilitate a purely anatomic hypotectomy.
Indocyanine green is applied to visually demarcate the line of ischemia on the liver surface, highlighting the area affected by the temporary clamping of the left hilar pedicle.
The surgical robot displays a coupled image of the intraoperative ultrasound within the same visual field, providing real-time imaging during the procedure.
During the left hepatectomy, the surgeon identifies the middle and left hepatic veins, noting the proximity of the tumor to the outlet of the middle hepatic vein.

The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma using robotic instruments that combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing. Maryland bipolar forceps are employed for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. As the partition progresses, polymer clips are applied to secure vessels and ducts.
The surgeon identifies the veins in liver segments IVB and IVA, performing meticulous cleaning to prepare for sealing and sectioning.

The surgeon uses a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line to section the left hilar pedicle, adjusting the staple size according to tissue thickness. Three-point-five millimeter staples are used for the pedicle, while two-point-five millimeter staples are used for the left hepatic vein. The maneuvers are performed with a short Pringle maneuver applied.

The surgeon places the surgical specimen into a plastic bag. Hemostasis of the liver parenchyma and staple section lines is visually checked. Indocyanine green is used to verify the absence of ischemic areas.
